title: “Here, monks, a monk reflects, reflects thus: ‘The various sufferings of many kinds that arise in the world, such as aging and death — what…
content:
“Here, monks, a monk reflects, reflects thus: ‘The various sufferings of many kinds that arise in the world, such as aging and death — what is their cause, what is their origin, what gives them birth, what brings them into existence? When what exists does aging and death come to be? When what does not exist does aging and death not come to be?’

Reflecting thus, he understands: ‘The various sufferings of many kinds that arise in the world, such as aging and death — birth is their cause, birth is their origin, birth gives them birth, birth brings them into existence. When birth exists, aging and death come to be. When birth does not exist, aging and death do not come to be.’”

title: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable…
content: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable by the ear … Odors cognizable by the nose … Tastes cognizable by the tongue … Tangibles cognizable by the body—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. These, monks, are the five strands of sensual pleasure. The pleasure and joy that arise dependent on these five strands of sensual pleasure—this is called sensual pleasure, defiled pleasure, worldling’s pleasure, ignoble pleasure. ‘I say that this kind of pleasure should not be pursued, should not be developed, should not be cultivated, should be feared.’”

title: “And what, monks, is non-restraint? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those forms,…
content: “And what, monks, is non-restraint? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those forms, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk, monks, should understand: ‘I have fallen away from wholesome states.’ The Tathāgata calls this ‘falling away.’ And, monks, there are sounds … odors … tastes … phenomena cognizable by the mind … If a monk delights in those phenomena, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk should understand: ‘I have fallen away from wholesome states.’ The Tathāgata calls this ‘falling away.’ Thus, monks, is non-restraint.”

title: “Udāyi, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable…
content: “Udāyi, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable by the ear … Odors cognizable by the nose … Tastes cognizable by the tongue … Tangibles cognizable by the body—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. These, Udāyi, are the five strands of sensual pleasure. Whatever pleasure or joy arises in dependence on these five strands of sensual pleasure, that is called sensual pleasure, defiled pleasure, worldling’s pleasure, ignoble pleasure. I say that such pleasure should not be pursued, should not be developed, should not be cultivated, should be feared.”

title: Behold this adorned appearance, With jewels and earrings, A skeleton covered by skin, Made beautiful by clothes.
content:
Behold this adorned appearance,
With jewels and earrings,
A skeleton covered by skin,
Made beautiful by clothes.

title: “From attachment arises grief, From attachment arises fear. For one freed from attachment, there’s no grief–so how fear?” “From delight arises grief, From delight arises…
content:
“From attachment arises grief,
From attachment arises fear.
For one freed from attachment,
there’s no grief–so how fear?”

“From delight arises grief,
From delight arises fear.
For one freed from delight,
there’s no grief–so how fear?”

“From sensual desire arises grief,
From sensual desire arises fear.
For one freed from sensual desire,
there’s no grief–so how fear?”

“From craving arises grief,
From craving arises fear.
For one freed from craving,
there’s no grief–so how fear?”

title: “Good sirs, what is the pursuit of the past? ‘My eye in the past was such and such, forms were such and such, and consciousness…
content: “Good sirs, what is the pursuit of the past? ‘My eye in the past was such and such, forms were such and such, and consciousness there was bound by desire and lust. Because consciousness is bound by desire and lust, he delights in that. Delighting in that, he pursues the past. My ear in the past … nose … tongue … body … mind in the past was such and such, phenomena were such and such, and consciousness there was bound by desire and lust. Because consciousness is bound by desire and lust, he delights in that. Delighting in that, he pursues the past.’ This, good sirs, is the pursuit of the past.”

title: - “Venerable sir, how did you cross the flood?” - “Friend, by not halting and not straining, I crossed the flood.” - “But how, venerable…
content:
- “Venerable sir, how did you cross the flood?”
- “Friend, by not halting and not straining, I crossed the flood.”
- “But how, venerable sir, did you cross the flood by not halting and not straining?”
- “When I halted, friend, I sank. When I strained, I was swept away. So, friend, by not halting and not straining, I crossed the flood.”
(The deity):
After a long time I see
A brahmin who is fully extinguished —
By not halting, not straining,
He has crossed all clinging in the world.

title: “For a monk practicing the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma, this is in accordance with the Dhamma: he dwells often in disenchantment toward form,…
content: “For a monk practicing the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma, this is in accordance with the Dhamma: he dwells often in disenchantment toward form, toward feeling, toward perception, toward formations, toward consciousness. Dwelling often in disenchantment toward form … toward consciousness, he fully understands form … feeling … perception … formations … consciousness. By fully understanding them, he is freed from form, freed from feeling, freed from perception, freed from formations, freed from consciousness, freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is freed from suffering.”

title: “Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms. Furthermore, monks, one sees that woman’s body thrown in the charnel ground, one, two, or…
content:
“Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms. Furthermore, monks, one sees that woman’s body thrown in the charnel ground, one, two, or three days dead – bloated, discolored, festering. What do you think, monks? Has the former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?”
“Yes, venerable sir.”
“Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms.”
